name: Canada
tactical_document:
# Canada — Tactical Profile

## Identity & Philosophy
Co-hosts of the 2026 World Cup and the rising power of CONCACAF. After the underwhelming 2022 group-stage exit, the Canadian Soccer Association brought in **Jesse Marsch** — a Red Bull-tree disciple of Ralf Rangnick — to install a **vertical, high-intensity, transitional** identity that suits the squad's athletic profile better than the slower possession game it had tried. Marsch's Canada is the most physically aggressive team in CONCACAF: high press, sprint duels, second balls, and direct passing into Jonathan David's runs. The team is **less technically refined** than the elite European sides but more athletic and arguably more dangerous in transition.

**Round of 16 (4 July, vs Morocco — Houston Stadium, Texas):** Canada are in the last 16 of a World Cup for the **first time in their history**. After a historic group (a first-ever World Cup point vs Bosnia, then a thumping **6-0 win over Qatar** with a Jonathan David hat-trick), a **1-2 loss to Switzerland** in the decider cost them top spot in Group B and home advantage — so instead of playing in Canada they beat **South Africa 1-0** in Los Angeles in the Round of 32, their first-ever World Cup knockout win, sealed by a stoppage-time goal. Now they travel to Houston to face a dangerous Morocco side that knocked out the Netherlands on penalties.

The knockout brings the tournament's biggest personnel story to a head: **Alphonso Davies is fit and STARTS** for the first time all tournament. After a hamstring strain kept him out of the group stage, Davies made a 15-minute cameo off the bench vs South Africa and Marsch has now unleashed him from the first whistle — deployed at **left-back**, where his engine and 1v1 threat weaponize the left flank while keeping the attacking structure ahead of him intact. His start pushes **Richie Laryea to the bench**. Two more changes freshen the front: **Liam Millar** takes the left-mid berth (over Ali Ahmed/Jacob Shaffelburg), and pacey target man **Tani Oluwaseyi** partners David up top in place of **Cyle Larin**. **Ismaël Koné remains OUT for the tournament** (broken leg vs Qatar); **Nathan Saliba** keeps the central-midfield berth. Yellow cards reset for the knockouts. Marsch keeps the **4-4-2** that hammered Qatar.

## Formation
- Shape: **4-4-2** — two flat banks of four with David and Oluwaseyi paired up top; morphs into a **4-2-2-2** in the high press and a **4-4-2 mid-block** when the press is broken. With Davies at LB the left becomes the lopsided superstar channel.
- Role mapping (roster order in `canada.yaml`):
  - index 0: GK — **Maxime Crépeau** — confirmed starter over St. Clair; agile shot-stopper, vocal organizer, willing to go long early.
  - index 1: LB — **Alphonso Davies** — **fit at last and starting** for the first time this tournament. Explosive (speed 19), a genuine 1v1 dribbler (dribbling 17); flies up the left touchline in transition and is Canada's single most dangerous runner. Deployed as a marauding fullback rather than a winger so the base back four holds.
  - index 2: LCB — **Moïse Bombito** — powerful, recovering athlete (strength 16) back from a tibia injury; aggressive front-foot defender who steps into the press, the senior physical presence of the back line, and the shield behind Davies's forward runs.
  - index 3: RCB — **Derek Cornelius** — disciplined line-holder and organizing CB; the more conservative half of the pairing who stays home when Davies bombs on.
  - index 4: RB — **Alistair Johnston** — Canada's first-choice right-back (Celtic, captain-grade leader); overlaps or inverts based on game state, disciplined positioning and high stamina. Tucks in to balance Davies's adventures on the left.
  - index 5: LM — **Liam Millar** — direct, dribbling left winger (dribbling 16, speed 16); likes to run at defenders and drift inside, freeing the outside lane for Davies's overlap. High-energy presser on the flank.
  - index 6: CM — **Nathan Saliba** — the central midfielder in for the injured Koné; box-to-box runner, set-piece weapon (scored a free kick vs Qatar), the press's middle layer.
  - index 7: CM — **Stephen Eustáquio** — the technical anchor. The lone deep playmaker; Marsch lets him sit because the rest of the midfield is verticality-and-running.
  - index 8: RM — **Tajon Buchanan** — pacy wide threat as the right mid in the 4-4-2; tucks inside to combine, leaving the overlap lane to Johnston.
  - index 9: ST — **Jonathan David** — **captain** and the team's chief finisher; runs the channels, peels off shoulders, the movement-first #9 who drops into pockets but always finishes the move.
  - index 10: ST — **Tani Oluwaseyi** — pacy, powerful target striker alongside David (speed 16, strength 15); leads the line, holds it up, stretches defences with his running, and offers a different aerial-and-pace threat than Larin — the wall for David to play off.

## Style of Play

### Build-up
**Direct.** Marsch's Canada does not enjoy long possession sequences. Crépeau often goes long from the goal kick aiming at Oluwaseyi's chest or into the channels for David. When building short, Eustáquio drops between the CBs to form a 3+1, the fullbacks (Davies and Johnston) push on in turn — never both at once — and Saliba offers the vertical bounce pass. **Three-pass goals are the dream** — keeper to midfielder to David to goal.

### Pressing
**High press is the identity.** This is Canada's defining trait. The 4-4-2 gives Marsch a natural two-striker press: David and Oluwaseyi split the CBs. Trigger #1: any back-pass to the GK — David sprints, Millar and Buchanan jump the fullbacks. Trigger #2: opposition CB receiving facing his own goal — David curves the run to block the back-pass, Saliba jumps. The press is **man-oriented** in all phases. Marsch wants the ball won in the opposition third **at all costs**, including bodily contact and tactical fouls — though against Morocco's technical Bouaddi–El Aynaoui pivot, with the midfield a man light after the Koné injury, Saliba must pick his pressing moments to avoid being outnumbered centrally.

### Defensive shape
When the press is broken, Canada drops into its base **4-4-2** with David alongside Oluwaseyi. Millar and Buchanan retreat onto the fullbacks. Davies is a supreme athlete but a positionally raw LB — Bombito must cover behind him, and the team never loses both fullbacks at once. The block is **mid-to-high**, never deep — Marsch refuses to invite pressure. Eustáquio shields while Saliba presses the half-spaces.

### Wide play
**Lopsided LEFT.** With Davies starting, the famous superstar-left identity is back on from kickoff. Millar (LM) drifts inside to combine in the half-space, and Davies overlaps OUTSIDE him on the sprint to provide the width — the single most dangerous channel Canada has, aimed straight at Achraf Hakimi's flank. On the right, Johnston overlaps Buchanan when Buchanan is wide, underlaps when Buchanan tucks inside, but stays more conservative to balance Davies's runs.

### Final third
Patterns: long ball from Crépeau or Eustáquio into the channel for David's first-time touch; Oluwaseyi pinning the CBs and flicking on for David's run; Buchanan running in behind onto a Eustáquio diagonal; Davies sprinting the left touchline and crossing low across the six-yard box for the two strikers. Canada does not pattern-make in 15-pass build-ups — the goals come from 3-4 pass transitions.

## Set Pieces
- Attacking corners: **Eustáquio** delivers from both sides (technical 16 pass rating). Primary aerial targets: Oluwaseyi (penalty spot), Bombito, Cornelius.
- Free kicks: **Eustáquio** delivers all wide free kicks; **Saliba** is a direct-attempt option from central range (free-kick scorer vs Qatar).
- Defending corners: **man-marking** with one zonal at the near post (Saliba). Bombito and Cornelius take the most dangerous opposing targets.
- Penalties: **David** primary, **Davies** secondary (penalty 12), **Eustáquio** tertiary.

## decide() Decision Priorities
Concrete rules the LLM should encode:
1. **If my player_id ends with "_1" (LB, jersey #19 — Davies) and team_phase == "attacking" and the LM "_5" (Millar) has tucked inside:** Sprint the overlap down the left touchline and cross low — Davies is the superstar transition runner (speed 19, dribbling 17). But recover on the sprint the instant possession is lost.
2. **If my player_id ends with "_1" (LB, jersey #19 — Davies) and team_phase == "defending" and the ball is on the right:** Hold a deeper LB position; do not push high — Davies is positionally raw, so avoid leaving Bombito exposed 2v1.
3. **If my role is FWD and an opponent (CB/GK) has just received the ball:** Sprint to press within 6 units within 3 ticks — David and Oluwaseyi split the two CBs.
4. **If my player_id ends with "_7" (CM, jersey #7 — Eustáquio) and I have the ball:** Prefer the long forward Pass (diagonal to RM "_8" Buchanan, through-ball to ST "_9" David, or the early diagonal into Davies's "_1" overlap) over the short recycle.
5. **If my player_id ends with "_9" (ST, jersey #10 — David) and team_phase == "transition_attack":** Sprint forward on the diagonal — Marsch's vertical philosophy requires the captain-striker to lead the break.
6. **If team has just won possession in the opposition third:** Vertical Pass to the nearest forward within 2 ticks. No recycle.
7. **If my role is GK (player_id ends with "_0" — Crépeau) and team has possession in our half:** Pass long to the target ST (player_id ends with "_10" — Oluwaseyi) if pressed, short to CB if not. Avoid medium-range balls (turnover risk).
8. **If my role is MID and an opponent has the ball within 7 units in opposition half:** Tackle — but if my player_id ends with "_6" (CM — Saliba), only commit when cover exists; the midfield is a man light, so do not get dragged out of the central screen.
9. **If my player_id ends with "_8" (RM, jersey #17 — Buchanan) and the RB "_4" (Johnston) is overlapping:** Tuck inside as a narrow second-line attacker between the lines.
10. **If team is leading by 1+ goals and minute > 80:** Drop into 4-5-1 ("_10" Oluwaseyi drops to midfield); kill the game with a compact block — see out the knockout.
11. **If my player_id ends with "_4" (RB, jersey #2 — Johnston) and the LB (player_id ends with "_1" — Davies) has pushed forward on the overlap:** stay home and tuck toward the centre — the back line never loses both fullbacks at once, and Davies is the one licensed to fly.
12. **Set pieces in attacking third / penalties:** defer to ST "_9" (David, penalties primary), CM "_7" (Eustáquio, all delivery and wide FKs); LB "_1" (Davies) is the secondary penalty option; CM "_6" (Saliba) takes central direct free kicks.

## Key Player Notes
- **Davies (idx 1):** Fit and starting at last after a group-stage hamstring strain. Canada's most dangerous player and the reason the left is the primary attacking channel — speed 19 and dribbling 17 make him lethal on the overlap and in transition. Deployed at LB, not winger, so the back four holds; Bombito covers behind. Secondary penalty taker.
- **David (idx 9):** Captain, primary finisher and penalty taker. Shoot rating 17 — the team's most clinical attacker. License to drop into pockets but always finishes the move.
- **Eustáquio (idx 7):** The lone deep playmaker. The team's pass rating leader (16) — the only player tasked with long-range diagonal distribution and wide dead-ball delivery.
- **Saliba (idx 6):** Koné's replacement in central midfield after the broken-leg injury. A box-to-box runner and free-kick threat (scored vs Qatar) who must balance the press with shielding a midfield that is now a man light.
- **Millar (idx 5):** Direct, dribbling left winger in for Ahmed/Shaffelburg; runs at defenders and drifts inside (dribbling 16), pairing with Davies to overload the left. Returned from an ACL injury during the qualifying cycle.
- **Oluwaseyi (idx 10):** Pacy, powerful target striker (speed 16, strength 15) in for Larin; leads the line, holds it up, stretches defences with his running and gives David a different kind of partner — speed as much as aerial presence.
- **Bombito (idx 2):** Powerhouse centre-back (strength 16) back to near-full fitness after a tibia injury; steps into the front-foot press, covers behind Davies's overlaps, and is a major aerial threat at attacking set pieces.

## Tournament Mindset
Canada under Marsch is the **most physically aggressive** team in the tournament. The press, the sprint duels, the tactical fouling — it is a Red Bull team in a Canada shirt. The group stage delivered historic firsts (first point, first win) and the Round of 32 delivered a first-ever knockout victory over South Africa — but as Group B runners-up, on the road in Houston rather than at home. The chief worries are the **thinner-than-ideal midfield** after Koné's tournament-ending injury (Saliba holds the central screen against a quality Moroccan pivot) and **stamina management** — Marsch's press demands 90-minute intensity, and a knockout can run to 120. The great carrot is finally in the XI: **Alphonso Davies starts**, unleashed at last to weaponize the left against **Morocco** — a side that beat the Netherlands on penalties and boasts Hakimi, Diaz and Saibari. Expect Canada to press Morocco's build-up, attack down the Davies–Millar left channel, score in transition through David and Oluwaseyi, and back their athleticism to win a one-off knockout against a Moroccan side chasing history of its own.
